summary: Jeff Atwood warns that compromised routers represent a serious and underappreciated security threat, having personally witnessed two tech-savvy friends fall victim to router-based attacks. Unlike traditional malware that targets individual devices, a compromised router can intercept and manipulate all traffic from every connected device, making it the ultimate man-in-the-middle attack. Even HTTPS traffic isn't fully safe when attackers can manipulate DNS and forge certificates. Atwood provides practical defensive measures for both home networks (buying quality routers, updating firmware, disabling WPS/uPNP) and public WiFi (using only HTTPS sites and VPNs). He frames this as an ominous preview of IoT security challenges, arguing that every connected device is a powerful computer requiring serious security attention.
key_points: Router malware is more dangerous than computer malware because it can compromise every device on the network simultaneously
Attacks often come from 'inside the house' - malicious websites scripting your browser to access your router's admin interface with default credentials
Even HTTPS traffic can be subverted through DNS manipulation and forged certificates on compromised routers
Consumer router security is generally poor, with features like WPS and uPNP creating unnecessary vulnerabilities that should be disabled
The proliferation of IoT devices dramatically expands the attack surface since each is a small, powerful computer requiring security attention
money_quotes: Every time you use WiFi, ask yourself: could I be connecting to the Internet through a compromised router with malware?
Router malware is the ultimate man-in-the-middle attack.
If you can manage to systematically infect common home and business routers, you can potentially compromise every computer connected to them.
You're being attacked from the soft, creamy inside.
The calls are coming from inside your house!
Default SSIDs just scream hack me, for I have all defaults and a clueless owner.
The Internet of Things is a bunch of tiny, powerful computers, too – and they need the same strong attention to security that our smartphones, laptops, and servers already enjoy.
key_insight: The router sitting in your home or office is a fully capable computer that can be weaponized against you, representing both the current reality and a warning sign for the broader Internet of Things.
